title: Making a pig wallow
subtitle: How to build and maintain a mud wallow to protect pigs from sunburn and overheating.
excerpt: Pigs cannot sweat. In warm weather, they rely on wet mud to stay cool and to protect their skin from sunburn. A wallow costs almost nothing to make and meets one of the clearest welfare needs of any outdoor pig.
sourceNotes: RSPCA welfare standards for pigs. AHDB Pork, outdoor pig welfare in summer. Five Freedoms framework; freedom from discomfort.

glossaryTerms.definition: Pigs have very few sweat glands and cannot regulate body temperature by sweating. They rely instead on seeking shade, using wallows, and panting. In hot weather, a pig that cannot access a wallow or shade will rapidly overheat. Pink-skinned breeds are also at risk of sunburn without a mud coating.

aliases: how to make a pig wallow

content.text: Mud wallowing is not an optional behaviour for pigs; it is a key mechanism of

. A pig coated in wet mud stays significantly cooler as the water evaporates. The mud layer also provides physical protection against biting flies and sunburn. In summer, a wallow is as important as clean water.

The simplest wallow is a depression in the ground that you keep wet. Choose a shaded spot if possible; a wallow in direct sun dries out quickly. Dig or allow the pigs to root a shallow depression, around 20 cm deep and 2 to 3 metres across for a group of three or four pigs. Fill it with water from a hose each morning in dry weather. Pigs will maintain and enlarge it themselves.

If your pigs are on hard standing or where a soil depression is not practical, use a large, low-sided container filled with a mixture of soil and water. A concrete mixing trough or a shallow galvanised trough filled with wet soil works well. Pigs will climb in, root, and wallow. Refill with fresh water daily and top up with soil as needed.

Site the wallow in or near available shade. A wallow under a tree or beside a shelter will dry more slowly and will be used more consistently. Without shade, pigs in hot weather will seek both: a shaded area first, then the wallow. If your paddock has no natural shade, provide a temporary sunshade or tarpaulin over the wallow area in midsummer.

A pig that is overheating will pant heavily, move very slowly, lie flat with legs splayed, or seek shade and lie motionless. In severe cases the ears may flush red. Hose pigs down with cool (not cold) water if a wallow is not accessible. Move them to shade and ensure water is available. Do not give a very hot pig ice-cold water to drink; the shock can cause problems. Steady cooling is safer than rapid cooling.
